--- SOURCE 4: Catalyst selection is the key to higher refinery performance ---
URL: https://www.catalyst-intelligence.com/blog/catalyst-selection-is-your-key-to-higher-refinery-performance

SUMMARY:
Catalyst selection is your key to improved refinery performance. Refineries are advised to start early to avoid a sub-optimal selection

FULL CONTENT:
![](/_assets/img/glass.png)
![logo](/website/Catalyst_Intelligence_logo_zonder_New.jpg)
![](/slider11.jpg)

# Catalyst selection is your key to higher refinery performance; start early!

# Catalyst selection is your key to higher refinery performance; start early!

Many refineries are postponing the selection of a new catalyst for the next change-out to the “last minute”. This behaviour often gives rise to a non-optimal
choice, lower unit performance and above average catalyst pricing. We asked ourselves; (i) why is it that some refiners start late on the catalyst
selection process, (ii) what are the consequences of this choice, and (iii) is there a better way?

## Why do some refineries start late on the catalyst selection process?

More urgent day-to-day tasks tend to keep everybody occupied while a catalyst change-out seems far away and it is a multi-disciplinary effort. Getting
everybody together to talk about future operating conditions and production objectives is difficult as future market conditions are unknown and it
is only natural to think that in three months from now we will have a better grasp of what the future will look like, thus trying to make a decision
just in time. Just in time from the refiners point of view may be just too late from the catalyst vendor’s point of view, as the vendor wants to limit
inventory costs and is operating his plant with a certain product lead time. It is not uncommon for a vendor to have a product lead time of 3 to 6
months for common products and even longer for certain specialties. Furthermore, the catalyst business is innovative, and it is not unlikely that one
of the vendors has a new product and is promising superior performance. Then, the question presents itself whether the refinery wants to be a Guiney
pig or that they want to test claims before deciding to buy this product. Doing a test takes a long lead time as capacity at third party laboratories
is scarce, and several tasks need to be completed before the test can be conducted including NDA and feedstock selection as well as agreement on the
protocol to be used. Finally, if a refinery is waiting for deactivation to set in before starting to look for a next batch they may be surprised by
the higher rate of deactivation towards the end of the cycle caused by regular operation or made worse by unit up-sets necessitating an even earlier
change-out than anticipated.

### What are the consequences of the late start of the catalyst selection?

### What can be done to have a better catalyst selection process?

First of all the refiner needs to realize that catalyst selection is a long process and that if you want to give it the attention it deserves one needs
to start early or even maintain a continuous screening process to be aware of developments in the catalyst market. This is especially true for large
units, such as ULSD and residue units, and units with a major impact on the refinery profitability such as hydrocrackers. To provide sufficient time
for all tasks involved in a proper selection a good recommendation would be to start 1.5 to 2 years before the next catalyst change-out. Monitoring
the performance of the unit is important so that the refinery knows what the catalyst does well and what it does not so well, this monitoring will
help to predict the end of cycle and also indicate areas where the refiner would like to see improvements based on operation of the catalyst in their
unit. Be precise in describing the unit constraints and the operating objectives, so that vendors have best available input data to feed their models.
Less ambiguity will result in accurate offers and better guarantees. Sharing monitoring data with the incumbent supplier may be a good idea in case
the vendor provide useful advice on how the refiner can optimize the current cycle. Performance of the refinery depends on the performance of the units
and a catalyst change-out is an opportunity to improve the performance of that particular unit. The performance targets for the next cycle should be
precisely formulated in the RFQ. This may be the processing of a more difficult feedstock or a longer cycle length (activity), or it may be a higher
selectivity towards desired products (selectivity), or a better resistance towards coking or poisoning (stability). Once the anticipated operating
conditions are clear (this can be several scenarios) and the targets are clear the vendor can optimize their offer. The vendor does not need to know
the financial impact of each optimization they propose, but the refinery needs to understand how catalyst performance is linked to future profitability
as that will drive the selection much more than catalyst price. Once the offers are in, it is time to compare the offers and decide which offers meet
the selection criteria best. Although catalyst vendor models are good, it may be worthwhile to perform a side-by-side test with the intended feedstock
and refinery operating conditions to verify the performance claims. Sometimes the results of a test differ significantly from a vendor prediction.
Testing by independent laboratories takes several months to prepare (NDA, feedstock, samples, agreement on protocol, unit availability) and one should
calculate with 6-9 months depending on the type of test. Groups of refineries can share the cost of catalyst selection and contract negotiation if
their units and operating objectives are similar. Pooling of future demand also gives leverage to refinery groups when they negotiate supply frame
agreements. A single refinery may decide to go the opposite way and develop a close relationship with the incumbent vendor and share performance data
and future objectives during the cycle. A better exchange of information allows an incumbent vendor to get to know the unit and the way the unit is
being operated by the refinery. This information can be used to assist the refinery on a regular basis as some vendors now start to do with connected
plants, where monitoring data are shared with the vendor, and feedback / recommendations are provided back to the refiner. This allows refineries to
make informed decisions based on big data and models owned and controlled by catalyst vendors / service providers. Finally, there is the possibility
to outsource the catalyst selection to an independent consultant who can help you formulate the future requirements, get a description of the likely
feedstock specifications and different scenarios and assist with testing, comparison, and even negotiation. Catalyst Intelligence has several independent
consultants with many years of experience in the refining industry and the catalyst business and we can extensive experience formulating precise performance
requirements, comparing test results and negotiating supply conditions.

### In summary: starting catalyst selection early creates opportunities to improve refinery profitability

Catalyst selection is your key to improved refinery performance. Refineries are advised to start early to avoid a sub-optimal selection resulting in reduced
refinery margins and above average catalyst costs. Based on our experience we recommend refineries to start with the selection some 1.5 to 2 years
before the expected catalyst change-out. Once the future operating objectives and selection criteria are set the rest will fall in place. It is important
to allow at least some 6 to 9 months for comparative testing and an additional 6 months product lead time from date of PO. It is easy to do this selection
yourself, but it is also possible to outsource this work to experienced consultants in case other urgent issues take up your staff time at the refinery.

Catalyst-Intelligence is a consulting company providing independent & comprehensive advice on fixed bed catalysts to refineries and petrochemical plants.
For more information on how we can help you we refer to our website: [www.catalyst-intelligence.com](http://www.catalyst-intelligence.com/)

# Everything You Need To Know About Test Catalyst

![How to Test Catalyst Quality? Everything You Need to Know](https://ledouxandcompany.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/test-catalyst-ledoux-1.webp)

Quality testing determines if catalysts will perform as intended in your industrial processes. The evaluation helps you identify performance issues before they affect production and make informed decisions about catalyst replacement or regeneration.

This guide covers the fundamentals to **test catalyst** quality – from proper sample preparation to standardized testing protocols. You’ll learn about standard testing methods, on-site monitoring techniques, and how to read test results accurately. Let’s examine the essential steps for evaluating catalyst quality and performance!

## **Why Do You Need to Test Catalyst?**

Regular catalyst testing helps pinpoint performance issues before they lead to costly production problems or product defects. Through proper testing, you can spot early signs of catalyst degradation and maintain consistent product quality.

### **Performance Evaluation**

Performance evaluation starts with testing new catalysts to verify they match the required specifications and standards. For used catalysts, testing reveals their remaining activity levels and helps determine the right time for regeneration or replacement. This systematic approach keeps production running smoothly and prevents unexpected shutdowns.

### **Process Optimization**

Testing data shapes process improvements by revealing the ideal operating conditions for temperature, pressure, and feed composition. The results point to opportunities for higher yields while controlling costs. Manufacturing teams can make informed adjustments based on concrete performance metrics rather than guesswork, leading to better production outcomes.

### **Troubleshooting**

When processes fall short of expectations, catalyst testing becomes an invaluable diagnostic tool. These tests help identify specific problems like deactivation patterns or poisoning effects, allowing teams to implement targeted solutions. Quick problem identification translates to less downtime and more consistent production quality.

### **Regulatory Compliance**

Many industries operate under strict environmental regulations and emissions standards. Regular catalyst testing provides the documentation needed to show compliance with these requirements. Testing records provide evidence for audits and ensure regulatory compliance.

### **Research and Development**

Testing drives progress in catalyst development and research. Scientists rely on test results to evaluate different formulations and compare various materials. This methodical experimentation supports the creation of better-performing catalysts that can transform industrial processes and open new possibilities for chemical manufacturing.

## **Guidelines for Preparing Catalyst Tests**

Thoughtful test catalyst preparation will ensure effective catalyst testing, which morally begins long before the actual tests are implemented.

Before starting catalyst testing, teams need clear goals that match their operational needs. These goals shape every aspect of testing, from choosing sampling methods to selecting specific test parameters. The main testing targets include measuring the catalyst’s ability to convert industrial materials and tracking its performance over time.

The testing process requires thoughtful sample selection to reflect the entire catalyst system. Take samples from steady points in the catalyst setup to maintain consistency and minimize exposure differences. Focus on gathering both new and used catalyst samples that match your production materials. Finally, match your test samples to your production catalysts in terms of materials and coatings to get meaningful comparisons.

Ensure the testing setup matches real-world operating conditions for meaningful results. For example, laboratory systems typically include a testing tube reactor with a furnace that recreates the exact temperature and pressure levels in industrial settings. In addition, the gas mixtures used in testing should mirror the actual plant environment, with matching component concentrations.

## **How to Test Catalyst? Catalyst Testing Processes**

Test catalysts undergo standardized methods and procedures to check their performance and meet industry standards.

![How to Test Catalyst? Catalyst Testing Processes](https://ledouxandcompany.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/test-catalyst-ledoux-2.webp)

### **Standard Testing Methods**

**[Catalyst testing](https://ledouxandcompany.com/catalyst-testing/)** follows standardized test procedures that produce repeatable, accurate, and comparable data. A basic setup consists of a tube reactor with a temperature-controlled furnace and mass flow controllers.

The reactor output connects directly to analytical instruments like gas chromatographs, FID hydrocarbon detectors, CO detectors, and FTIR systems. The tube reactor setup can replicate EPA Test Method 25A protocols for emissions testing.

### **On-site Testing**

Stack testing measures catalyst performance directly in the operating system. Professional testing firms conduct these evaluations on-site, which costs more than lab analysis of catalyst samples. The main benefit comes from observing how the catalyst functions in real working conditions.

Though on-site evaluation reveals performance data in actual conditions, sending catalyst samples to specialized labs often yields more detailed and precise results. Lab testing allows for controlled conditions and thorough analysis using calibrated instruments, providing a clear picture of catalyst properties and capabilities.

### **Interpreting Catalyst Test Outcomes**

Proper analysis of test results helps manufacturers make sound decisions about catalyst performance and meet emissions standards.

Data collection tracks key measurements during testing. The process records temperature and pressure conditions along with VOC concentrations at input and output points.

The conversion rate shows the percentage of VOCs transformed. Product selectivity reveals the ratio of desired to unwanted outputs. Meanwhile, long-term stability measures activity levels through time.

Analysis methods reveal performance patterns. Statistical tools determine result reliability. Then, benchmark comparisons measure performance against standards. Mathematical modeling predicts reaction behavior under varying conditions.

The quality of catalyst testing directly impacts industrial outcomes and costs. Ledoux & Co., an **[ISO 17025 accredited laboratory](https://ledouxandcompany.com/metallurgical-analysis-services/what-is-iso-17025-accredited-laboratory/)**, conducts precise catalyst analysis through proven methods and equipment. Our **test catalyst** capabilities help manufacturers make informed decisions about catalyst performance and selection. This independent verification supports process improvements and meets regulatory standards across chemical and petrochemical sectors.

At Ledoux, we analyze catalysts through rigorous methods. Our lab specializes in testing spent automotive, industrial, reforming, fresh, EO, and VAM catalysts. Each sample undergoes thorough examination for precious metal content and material composition.

## **Ensure Precision and Accuracy in Catalyst Testing with Ledoux**

Thorough catalyst testing gives you clear insights into performance and helps prevent costly production issues. The right testing methods and equipment make the difference between reliable data and uncertain results.

For precise, accurate catalyst testing backed by decades of expertise, contact **[Ledoux & Company](https://ledouxandcompany.com/catalyst-testing/)**. Our laboratory teams understand the nuances of catalyst performance analysis and deliver detailed, actionable reports.

Our testing delivers commercial-grade documentation, precise PGM analysis, quality assessment reports, and fast turnaround times. Trust our 45+ years of catalyst testing experience for clients worldwide. We maintain strict quality controls and data security throughout the testing process.

DESCRIPTION: Selecting the right catalyst can significantly improve plant efficiency and reduce costs, with performance metrics such as turnover, stability, and cost playing crucial roles in the decision-making process.

URL: https://image.slidesharecdn.com/syngascatalystselectioncriteria-130729072100-phpapp02/85/Syngas-Catalyst-Selection-Criteria-5-638.jpg
DESCRIPTION: A presentation slide titled "Selecting Key Performance Criteria" lists considerations such as activity, pressure drop, and strength, emphasizing the importance of efficiency, stability, and cost in catalyst selection.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Key performance metrics include conversion rates, selectivity, and activity stability. Tailoring these metrics allows for optimal catalyst evaluation based on specific operational goals.
Catalyst stability is crucial for sustained performance. Assessing reusability through metrics like the stability number ensures long-term viability and minimizes replacement costs during operations.

Evaluating cost-effectiveness involves analyzing initial investment against operational savings. A lower-priced catalyst may incur higher operational costs, impacting overall profitability and sustainability.
